landing.heroSubtitle: Generate, sign, and send waivers that comply with your state's law. In Spanish, in minutes, from any device.
landing.heroBadge: Free — 3 waivers per month, no credit card required
landing.howItWorksSubtitle: Three simple steps to protect your right to get paid
landing.step1Description: Add project details, the general contractor, and contract value. Takes just a minute.
landing.step2Description: Select the waiver type, enter the amount, and our AI verifies everything is correct.
landing.step3Description: Digitally sign from your phone and send the PDF directly to the general contractor.
landing.featuresTitle: Everything you need to protect your payment
landing.featuresSubtitle: Tools designed for subcontractors who want to get paid fairly
landing.feature1Description: Official statutory forms for California and Georgia. Approved templates for Texas, Florida, and New York.
landing.feature2Description: Reviews amounts, dates, and waiver type before you sign. Alerts you if something doesn't add up.
landing.feature3Description: Sign from your phone or computer. Your signature is included directly in the PDF.
landing.feature4Description: Send the waiver with PDF attachment to the contractor's email. Without leaving the app.
landing.feature5Description: The entire platform in Spanish with the option to switch to English. As it should be.
landing.feature6Description: Private storage with temporary links. No one else can see your documents.
landing.statesComingSoon: Your state isn't listed? We'll be adding more soon.
landing.pricingSubtitle: Start free, upgrade when you're ready
landing.faq1Answer: A lien waiver is a legal document where you waive your lien rights (the right to place a claim on the property) in exchange for payment. It's common in construction: the general contractor asks for one before paying you, to ensure you won't place a lien on the property.
landing.faq2Answer: Yes. In all 5 states we support (California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York), digital waivers with electronic signatures are legally valid. For California and Georgia, we use the official statutory forms required by law.
landing.faq3Question: Do I need a credit card?
landing.faq3Answer: No. The free plan doesn't require a credit card. You can generate up to 3 waivers per month at no cost. You only need an email address to sign up.
landing.faq4Answer: We currently support California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York. For California and Georgia, we generate official statutory forms. For Texas, Florida, and New York, we use professional templates that follow each state's standard practices.
landing.faq5Answer: The AI validates your waiver by checking amounts, dates, and document type, but it never blocks you. If it detects something unusual, it shows you a warning so you can decide. You always have final control over your document.
landing.faq6Answer: Yes. All your documents are stored on a private server with temporary links (signed URLs). Only you can access your waivers. We don't share your information with third parties.

conditional_progress.name: Conditional Waiver — Progress Payment
conditional_progress.description: Use when requesting a progress payment and you have NOT yet been paid. Your lien rights are only waived IF and WHEN payment clears.
conditional_progress.risk: Low Risk — Your rights are protected until payment is received

unconditional_progress.name: Unconditional Waiver — Progress Payment
unconditional_progress.description: Use ONLY when you have already received and deposited a progress payment. This waiver takes effect IMMEDIATELY upon signing, whether or not the check clears.
unconditional_progress.risk: Medium Risk — Make sure you have payment in hand before signing

conditional_final.name: Conditional Waiver — Final Payment
conditional_final.description: Use when requesting your final payment and you have NOT yet been paid. Your rights are only waived when full payment clears.
conditional_final.risk: Low Risk — Protected until final payment is received

unconditional_final.name: Unconditional Waiver — Final Payment
unconditional_final.description: Use ONLY when you have received your complete final payment. This permanently waives ALL lien rights on the project. There is no going back.
unconditional_final.risk: High Risk — You permanently lose ALL rights. Only sign if fully paid.

stateGuides.indexMetaDescription: Complete lien waiver guides for California, Georgia, Texas, Florida, and New York. Learn your state's legal requirements.
stateGuides.indexSubtitle: Understand your state's legal requirements to generate compliant lien waivers
stateGuides.metaDescription: Complete guide to lien waivers in {stateName}. Legal requirements under {statute}, waiver types, required fields, and more.
stateGuides.heroSubtitle: Complete guide to lien waiver requirements
stateGuides.ctaTitle: Ready to create your {stateName} lien waiver?
stateGuides.ctaSubtitle: Generate your waiver in minutes with forms that comply with {stateName} law
stateGuides.CA_overview: California has the strictest lien waiver requirements in the country. State law (Cal. Civ. Code §§ 8132-8138) requires waivers to use specific statutory forms — generic templates are not permitted. Each form includes mandatory NOTICE text that must appear in type at least as large as the largest type in the form. California supports all 4 waiver types: conditional and unconditional, for both progress and final payments.
stateGuides.CA_faq1Answer: California requires specific statutory forms defined in Cal. Civ. Code §§ 8132-8138. Waivers must be "substantially" the same as the forms provided by law. ObraKit automatically generates the correct California forms.
stateGuides.CA_faq2Question: What is the mandatory NOTICE text?
stateGuides.CA_faq2Answer: Every waiver form in California must include NOTICE text that warns about the rights being waived. This text must appear in type at least as large as the largest type in the form. ObraKit includes it automatically.
stateGuides.CA_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in California?
stateGuides.CA_faq3Answer: Yes. California recognizes electronic signatures under the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA) and the federal E-SIGN Act. Electronically signed waivers have the same legal validity as paper-signed ones.
stateGuides.GA_overview: Georgia requires statutory forms for lien waivers under O.C.G.A. § 44-14-366. Unlike other states, Georgia only recognizes 2 waiver types: Interim Waiver (progress payment) and Final Waiver. The Interim Waiver has a special rule: it is conditional for 90 days, then automatically converts to unconditional unless an Affidavit of Nonpayment is filed. Forms must use a minimum 12-point font size.
stateGuides.GA_faq1Answer: In Georgia, the Interim Waiver (progress payment waiver) is conditional for the first 90 days. If you don't file an Affidavit of Nonpayment within that period, the waiver automatically converts to unconditional. It's important to track payments within this timeframe.
stateGuides.GA_faq2Question: What is the minimum font size requirement?
stateGuides.GA_faq2Answer: Georgia requires lien waiver forms to use a minimum 12-point font size. ObraKit automatically generates all Georgia PDFs with the correct font size.
stateGuides.GA_faq3Answer: No. Georgia does not require lien waivers to be notarized. A valid electronic signature is sufficient for the document to be legally binding.
stateGuides.TX_overview: Texas does not require statutory forms for lien waivers. Under Tex. Prop. Code §§ 53.281-53.286, waivers are valid as long as they clearly identify the project, amount, and scope of rights being waived. Texas supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit uses professional templates that comply with Texas standard practices.
stateGuides.TX_faq1Answer: No. Texas does not have mandatory statutory forms for lien waivers. However, the waiver must clearly identify the project, payment amount, and rights being waived. ObraKit uses professional templates that cover all requirements.
stateGuides.TX_faq2Question: What are lien rights in Texas?
stateGuides.TX_faq2Answer: In Texas, subcontractors have the right to file a mechanic's lien on the property if they don't receive payment. By signing a waiver, you give up this right to the extent of the specified amount. It's important to understand the difference between conditional and unconditional waivers.
stateGuides.TX_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in Texas?
stateGuides.TX_faq3Answer: Yes. Texas recognizes electronic signatures under the Texas Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (TUETA). Digitally signed waivers are legally valid.
stateGuides.FL_overview: Florida does not have statutory forms for lien waivers but has detailed lien law under Fla. Stat. §§ 713.01-713.37. Waivers are enforceable if properly signed. Florida supports all 4 waiver types. Florida's law has specific rules about deadlines for filing liens, making it important to generate waivers with the correct dates.
stateGuides.FL_faq1Answer: Florida has complex lien law with specific notification requirements and deadlines. While it doesn't require statutory waiver forms, it's important that the waiver clearly states the rights being waived. ObraKit generates waivers that comply with Florida standard practices.
stateGuides.FL_faq2Answer: No. Florida does not require lien waivers to be notarized. However, a valid signature is essential for the document to be enforceable.
stateGuides.FL_faq3Question: What are the important deadlines in Florida?
stateGuides.FL_faq3Answer: In Florida, subcontractors must send a Notice to Owner within the first 45 days of starting work. The lien must be filed within 90 days after the last date of work. It's important to have correct dates on your waivers.
stateGuides.NY_overview: New York does not have statutory forms for lien waivers. Under N.Y. Lien Law §§ 3-39-a, waivers are generally enforceable when voluntarily signed. New York supports all 4 waiver types. ObraKit uses professional templates that follow New York's standard industry practices.
stateGuides.NY_faq1Question: Does New York require specific forms?
stateGuides.NY_faq1Answer: No. New York does not have mandatory statutory forms for lien waivers. The waiver should clearly state the rights being waived and the payment amount. ObraKit generates professional templates that meet New York industry standards.
stateGuides.NY_faq2Question: How do mechanic's liens work in New York?
stateGuides.NY_faq2Answer: In New York, subcontractors can file a mechanic's lien within 8 months after completing work (4 months for single-family homes). By signing a waiver, you give up this right to the extent specified.
stateGuides.NY_faq3Question: Are electronic signatures valid in New York?
stateGuides.NY_faq3Answer: Yes. New York recognizes electronic signatures under the New York Electronic Signatures and Records Act (ESRA). Digitally signed waivers are legally valid.
